chunk_summary: Case-based management of recurrent pneumonia in an 8-year-old with spastic quadriplegic CP (GMFCS V), focusing on feeding safety, secretion control, and nutrition adequacy, with interim strategies and tube escalation considerations.
learning_objectives: Identify high-risk feeding signs in children with neurodisability, including aspiration-related respiratory events and prolonged mealtimes.
Develop interim feeding strategies to improve swallow safety, hydration, and nutrition while minimizing aspiration risk.
Understand the role of NGT bridge trials and gastrostomy in managing unsustainable feeding burdens and recurrent pneumonia.
key_facts: The patient experienced three antibiotic-treated pneumonias in 12 months, indicating high aspiration risk.
Meals lasted 75–90 minutes, with weight plateauing for 8 months, highlighting poor feeding efficiency.
Interim strategies include IDDSI-tested thickened liquids, 90–90–90 positioning, reduced bolus size, and meal duration capped at 30 minutes.
NGT bridge trials (2–4 weeks) are recommended to assess proof-of-benefit before gastrostomy decisions.
Thickened fluids may reduce coughing but can worsen thirst, constipation, and medication delivery, requiring close monitoring.
reference_quote: ‘Gastrostomy tube feeding should be considered when oral feeding is unsafe or insufficient to meet hydration and nutritional needs.’ (Source: https://cps.ca/en/documents/position/gastrostomy-tube-feeding)
document_key_topics: NGT bridge trials and gastrostomy planning
Aspiration risk management in pediatric care
